concepts.title: Strength of an acid
concepts.conceptBody:
Strength of an acid

The conversion of carboxylic acids and phenols to carboxylate or phenolate ions is common because the proton is acidic, thus the proton is easily removed to form the anion in weakly basic medium. Most organic compounds are much less acidic than carboxylic acids, and thus need stronger basic medium to ionize a carbon-hydrogen bond. Because of the high electronegativity of a fluorine atom, fluorinated compounds are always more acidic than non-fluorinated compounds.

Strength of an Acid : The strength of an acid HA in water may be determined by considering the equilibrium:

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+\text{HA}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{\oplus }}+{{\text{A}}^{\ominus }}$

The equilibrium constant, in water is given by:

$\text{Ka}=\frac{\left[ {{\text{H}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}^{+}} \right]\left[ {{\text{A}}^{-}} \right]}{[\text{HA}]}$

It should be emphasized that $\text{Ka}$, the acidity constant of the acid in water, is only approximate (as above) if concentrations are used instead of the more correct activities. The smaller the numerical value of $\text{pKa}$, the stronger the acid to which it refers.

The origin of acidity in organic compounds

Among the factors that may influence the acidity of an organic compound, HA, are:

(a) The strength of the H-A bonds.

(b) The electronegativity of A.

(c) Factors stabilising ${{\text{A}}^{-}}$compared with HA.

(d) The nature of the solvent.

Of these (a) is not normally found to be a limiting factor, but the effect of (b) is reflected in the fact that the pKa of methanol, $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{O}-\text{H}$, is $=16$ while that of methane, $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ is = 43, oxygen being considerably more electronegative than carbon. Much more important is (c): the stabilisation is possible in the methanoate anion compared with the undissociated methanoic acid molecule results in the pKa of formic acid being 3.77 .

This is extremely effective delocalisation, with consequent stabilisation, in the methanoate anion involving as it does two canonical structures of identical energy.

Phenols are indeed found to be stronger acids than alcohols (the pKa of phenol itself is 9.95) but considerably weaker than carboxylic acids. This is due to the fact that in carboxylic acids, delocalisation of the negative charge in the carboxylate anion involves structures of identical energy and the atoms holding the negative charge are the highly electronegative oxygen atoms; whereas in the phenoxide anion, the structures involving negative charge on the carbon atoms are likely to be higher energy content than the one in which it is on oxygen and, in addition, of the centres carrying negative charge, only one structure is carrying highly electronegative oxygen atom.

Influence of the solvent:

The prime requirement of the solvent is that it should be capable of functioning as a base; the weaker the base, the smaller the dissociation of the acid. Thus we find that in, for example, methylbenzene (toluene) $\text{HCl}$ occurs as such, i.e., it is almost fully undissociated.

Factors affecting the strength of acids and bases

[A] Effect of structure:

1. Field effect $(-\text{I}$ effesct and $+\text{I}$ effect)

2. Resonance effect

3. Periodic Table correlations

4. Steric hinderance

5. Hybridisation

6. Statistical Effects: In a symmetrical diprotic acid, the first dissociation constant is twice as large as expected, since there are two equivalent ionizable hydrogens, while the second constant is only half as large as expected because the conjugate base can accept a proton at two equivalent sites. So ${{\text{k}}_{1}}/{{\text{k}}_{2}}$ should be 4 . Similar arguments hold good for basic groups.

7. Hydrogen Bonding: 

p-hydroxy benzoic acid $>0-$ hydroxy benzoic acid

$\text{p}{{\text{k}}_{\text{a}}}=4.58$ $\text{p}{{\text{k}}_{\text{n}}}=2.98$

$\left( {{\text{k}}_{\text{a}}}={{10}^{-5}} \right)$ $\left( {{\text{k}}_{\text{n}}}={{10}^{-3}} \right)$

Intra molecular hydrogen bonding between the $\text{OH}$ and $\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}$groups of the conjugate base of the ortho isomer stablises it and results in an increased activity.

[B] The Effect of Medium or Solvent

(a) If a base is more solvated than its conjugate acid, its stability is increased relative to the conjugate acid. e.g.

$\text{MeN}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{. }$

(b) Aniline in the gaseous phase is a stronger base than$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ but its much lower basicity in aqueous solution is caused by similar solvation effects and not by resonance and field electron with drawing effect of a phenyl group.

Similarly,

Pyridine and pyrrole $<\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ (In aqueous solution)

Pyridine and pyrrole $>\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ (In aqueous phase)

(c) For simple alcohols, following order of acidity is due to solvation effect.

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}>\text{MeC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}>\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{CHOH}>\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{3}}\text{COH (in solution) }$

${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}>\text{MeC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}<\text{MeCHOH}>\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{3}}\text{COH (in gaseous phases) }$

(d) A change from a protic to an aprotic solvent can also effect the acidity or basicity because there is a difference in solvation of anions by a protic solvent and an aprotic one e.g.

In $\text{DMF}$: Picric acid is stronger than $\text{HBr}$

In water: $\text{HBr}$ is stronger than picric acid

Relative Strength of Organic Acids

1. % S-character 

(a) Hydro carbons 

Hydrogen atom attached to sp-hybridised carbon is more acidic than that on sp 2 hybridised carbon which in turn is more acidic than that on sp3.

In other words, acidity increases with percentage (%) s-character. This is because, higher the character closer the electrons to the nucleus of $\text{C}$ and farther the electrons go from $\text{H}$, and easily can $\text{H}$ be removed as ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$.

Thus,

$\underset{\text{sp s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,s{{p}^{3}}}{\mathop{\text{HC}\equiv \text{CH}>{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}\equiv \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{H}{{\text{p}}_{3}}\text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,$

The factors responsible for influencing the acidity of an organic compound, $\text{H}-\text{X}$ are

(a) the strength of the $\text{H}-\text{X}$ bond 

(b) the electronegativity of $\mathbf{X}$ 

(c) factors stabilizing ${{\text{X}}^{-}}$as compared to $\text{HX}$ and 

(d) the nature of the solvent. 

The most important factors among all is (c). More stable (less basic) the conjugate base is, more acidic the compound will be. On comparing the acidic strength of two compounds, the compound having greater acidity will have higher value of ${{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ and lower $\text{p}{{\text{K}}_{\text{a}}}$ value.

Now let us see using any of these factors, how we can predict the order of acidic strength for organic acids.

(i) Alcohols: If we were to predict the order of acidic strength of ethanol, iso-propanol and tertiary butanol, we can proceed as

As alkyl groups (methyl group) have a $+\text{I}$ effect, the electron density at the oxygen atom will increase. More the number of alkyl groups, more will be the intensity of negative charge on oxygen. Greater the negative charge on the oxygen atom, the closer is the electron pair in the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond driven to the hydrogen atom and consequently separation of a proton becomes increasingly difficult. Thus, the acidic strength of given alcohols will be in the order

Ethanol > iso-propanol > t-butanol

In general, electron releasing groups decreases the acidity while electron withdrawing group increases the acidity of a compound. 

Substituted Phenols

The effect of a ring substituent on the acid strength depends on:

(1) Substituted group is electron attracting or releasing: electron withdrawing groups increases its acidity and vice-versa.

(2) Ability of the group to enter into resonance with the hydroxyl group and its position.

(3) Steric effect and hydrogen bonding due to the substituted group.

Illustration 

Compare acidity of

Solution: 

The methyl group is electron releasing and so release of a lone pair from oxygen (of the OH group in the unionized phenol or from the ${{\text{O}}^{-}}$phenoxide ion) into the ring is opposed. This results in diminished resonance in the contributing structures and consequently the phenoxide ion is more resonance stabilized with respect to phenol than is the methyl phenoxide ion with respect to methyl phenol. Hence, phenol is stronger acid than all methyl phenols. Thus, the order of acidity is

$\text{ Now among o},\text{p},\text{m methyl phenols, meta methyl phenol is most acidic }$

Now it is clear that meta methyl phenol are more acidic than ortho and para after the above structure. Now in case of o-methyl phenol, methyl group is closer to $\text{O}$ of $\text{OH}$ group in phenol than compared to $\text{p}$-methyl phenol. As a result it show more $+\text{I}$ effect, hence decreasing the acidic character.

concepts.title: Aliphatic and aromatic carboxylic acids
concepts.conceptBody:
Aliphatic carboxylic acids

The aliphatic carboxylic acids are much stronger acids than phenols and alcohols. This is attributed to the fact that the carboxylate ion (obtained by the loss of proton from carboxylic acid) is relatively more stabilized by delocalization than the phenoxide and alkoxide ions with respect to their undissociated molecules.

The delocalization of the negative charge in the carboxylate anion involves structures of identical energy content (as the negative charge resides on more electronegative atom, oxygen) while in phenoxide anion, the negative charge also resides on the less electronegative atom (carbon) making these structures of high energy content with respect to those structures in which the negative charge is on oxygen.

The presence of electron-withdrawing substituents in simple aliphatic acids increases their acidity while the electron-releasing substituents have reverse effect. For instance, let us compare the acidic strength of fluoroacetic acid and acetic acid.

The fluoro acetate ion is stabilized more due to strong-I effect of fluorine, with respect to acetate ion. Hence, fluoro acetate ion is less basic than acetate ion, thereby making fluoroacetic acid stronger than acetic acid.

Now, we can also compare the acidic strength of $\alpha $-and $\beta $-halo-substituted acids i.e.

Here the $\beta $-substituted compound will have less $-I$ effect as compared to $\alpha $-substituted acid and thus will be less acidic than b-substituted acid. Hence, the acidic strength is

$\text{F}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{COOH}>\text{F}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{COOH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{COOH}$

If there is a doubly bonded carbon atom adjacent to the carboxyl group, the acid strength is increased. This will be evident if we compare the acid strength of propanoic acid and propenoic (acrylic) acid.

As we know that unsaturated $\alpha $-carbon atom in propenoate ion is $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ hybridised, which means that electrons are drawn closer to the carbon nucleus than in a saturated, $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}$ hybridised $\alpha $-carbon atom, due to greater s-contribution in the $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ hybrid orbital. The result is that $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ hybridised carbon atoms are less electron-donating than saturated hybridised ones, thus, propanoate ion will be comparatively less stabilized than propenoate ion, thereby making propenoic acid stronger than propanoic acid (but propanoic will still be weaker than methanoic acid).

It there is a triple bonded carbon atom adjacent to carboxyl group, the acid strength is more with respect to the presence of a doubly bonded carbon atom adjacent to $-\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}$ group. This is attributed to the fact that $\text{sp}$ hybridized carbon atoms are less electron-releasing than $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ hybridized carbon atoms.

Some other trends of acidity are:

$\text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}>\ldots \ldots \ldots \ldots .......$

$\text{H}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}>\text{H}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}>\text{H}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}>\ldots \ldots \ldots .$

These trends reflect that in any homologous series, when we move from lower members to higher members, the acidity decreases.

Aromatic carboxylic acids

It has been pointed out that replacement of the hydrogen atom in formic acid by an alkyl group weakens the strength of the acid and the greater the $+\text{I}$ effect of the $\text{R}$ group, the weaker is the acid. Phenylacetic acid, $\text{PhC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\text{H}$ is stronger than acetic acid and therefore the phenyl group has an overall-I effect. On the other hand, benzoic acid is weaker than formic acid. In this case, the phenyl group has an overall releasing effect (which is smaller than that of methyl group). These apparently contradictory results may be explained as follows. When the carboxyl group is directly attached to the nucleus, the resonance effect $(+R)$ overcomes the $-\text{I}$ effect (in phenylacetic acid, the phenyl group is insulated from the carboxyl group by a $\text{CH}2$ group and so the $+\text{R}$ effect is not possible):

This prevents to a large extent, the lone pair on the $\text{O}$ atom of the $\text{OH}$ group from entering into resonance with the $\text{CO}$ group. The result is a smaller positive charge on the $\text{O}$ atom of the $\text{OH}$ group and so proton release is more difficult than in formic acid. The fact that benzoic acid is stronger than acetic acid means that $[-\text{I}+(+\text{R})]<[+\text{I}+(+\text{R})]$ of the methyl group.

The same arguments may be applied to ionized benzoic acid.

The ortho-effect. This is a steric effect that is shown by o-substituents. Nearly all o-substituted benzoic acids are stronger than benzoic acid due to this ortho effect irrespective of the polar nature of the o-substituent.

As we have seen that benzoic acid is a resonance hybrid and so the carboxyl group is coplanar with the ring. An o-substituent tends to prevent this coplanarity. Thus, resonance is diminished (or prevented) and so the $\text{O}$ atom of the $\text{OH}$ group has a greater positive charge, resulting in increased acid strength. It follows from this that greater the steric inhibition of resonance, the stronger is the acid. Support for this is the following order of strengths of substituted benzoic acids.

$\text{ 2, 6-di-Me }>2\text{-t-Bu }>2-\text{Me}$

Here again, if we consider the stability of the anion, steric inhibition of resonance prevents the $+R$ effect of the ring coming into operation and since this weakens acid strength, its absence results in increased acid strength.

O-Hydroxybenzoic acid (salicylic acid) is far stronger than the corresponding m-and p-isomers. Steric inhibition of resonance cannot explain this very large increase, since the corresponding methoxybenzoic acids all have almost similar strengths. The carboxylate ions of o-hydroxybenzoic acid is stabilised by intramolecular hydrogen bonding while such hydrogen bonding is not feassible in omethoxy benzoic acid and support for this is given by the following order of acid strength,

$2,6-\text{di}-\text{OH}>2-\text{OH}>\text{ benzoic acid }$

It can be seen that two hydrogen bonds would be expected to bring about more stabilization than one hydrogen bonds.

Illustration

Compare acidic strength of:

Solution: 

Due to the ortho-effect as explained above,

Now let us consider the effect of substituents from m-and p-positions. First let us take methyl substituted benzoic acids. As the methyl group has$+\text{I}$ effect, so the net result will be to increase the $+R$ effect in the $\text{m}-$ or $\text{p}$-tolyl group. Since, the inductive effect decreases with distance, so the $+\text{I}$ effect of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ from para position would be less than the $+\text{I}$ effect of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ group from meta position. Thus, the electron-density in the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond of m-methyl benzoic acid would be greater than that in p-methyl benzoic acid. Accordingly, the p-isomer should be more acidic than the $\text{m}$-isomer. But in real practice, the order is reverse. Actually, we have ignored the hyperconjugation effect of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ group. Due to hyperconjugation effect of $\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ in p-isomer, the electron density in the $\text{O}-\text{H}$ bond would be increased greatly with respect to that in $\text{m}$-isomer.

Thus, the release of proton is facilitated easily in m-isomer than is p-isomer.

Thus, the final order of acidity

concepts.title: Strength of organic base
concepts.conceptBody:
Strength of organic base

Strength of bases is related to the ease of accepting a proton, which in turn depends on the availability of electron pair on the nitrogen atom (or some other basic atom). More is the availability of electron pair, more easily the proton will be accepted and more will be the basic strength. If we compare the basicities of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}},\text{MeN}{{\text{H}}_{2}},\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{NH}$ and $\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{3}}~\text{N}$, then at a glance it would seem like $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}<\text{MeN}{{\text{H}}_{2}}<\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{NH}<\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{NH}<\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{3}}~\text{N}$ is the basicity order but the result is quite different in aqueous media. The correct order is $\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{NH}>\text{MeN}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{3}}~\text{N}>\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$. This order can be explained considering the solvation effect of the protonated amines. But in absence of a polar protic solvent i.e. in the presence of non-polar solvent or gaseous phase, the order of basicity is $\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{3}}~\text{N}>\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{NH}>\text{MeN}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$.

The effect of introducing electron withdrawing groups like $\text{Cl},\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$, $\text{RCO}$ group close to a basic centre decreases the basicity, due to their electron withdrawing inductive effect.

In aniline, owing to resonance, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is less available for co-ordinating with a proton and at the same time, small positive charge on the nitrogen atom would tend to repel a proton. Alternatively, since there are more resonating structures possible for aniline itself than for the cation ${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$, the former will be stablised with respect to the latter.

Aniline is a weaker base than ammonia or cyclohexylamine. It is because of the fact that the electron pair on nitrogen in involved in delocalization, making it less available for donation.

Diphenylamine is even a weaker base than aniline due to the presence of another phenyl group

and triphenylamine $\left( \text{P}{{\text{h}}_{3}}~\text{N} \right)$ is not basic at all by any means.

Introduction of alkyl group (like $\text{Me}$ ) on the nitrogen atom of aniline results in small increase in the basic strength.

${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}<{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Me}<{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{NM}{{\text{e}}_{2}}$

Unlike such introduction in aliphatic amines, this small increase in basic strength is progressive, indicating that cation stabilization through hydrogen-bonded solvation, here as less influence on the overall effect.

The effect of a ring substitutent on basicity depends on whether the substituent is electron attracting or releasing, its ability to enter into resonance with the amino-group and its position. All the nitroanilines are weaker bases than aniline. The nitro-group has a strong$-\text{R}$ effect and $\text{o}-$ and $\text{p}-$ nitroaniline are therefore, more resonance stabilised than aniline itself.

A m-nitro group cannot enter into resonance with the amino-group, but nevertheless m-nitroaniline is a much weaker base than aniline. In this case, nitro-group has a strong-I effect. This $-\text{I}$ effect tends to draw into the ring the lone pair of $\text{N}$, thus decreasing its basicity. Thus, the order of basicity of nitroanilines is ${{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{m}-\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{p}-\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>0-\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{6}}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$.

Tetraalkylammonium salts, e.g. ${{\text{R}}_{4}}~{{\text{N}}^{+}}{{\text{I}}^{-}}$on treatment with moist silver oxide (AgOH) yield basic solutions comparable in strength with the mineral alkalis. This is readily understandable as ${{\text{R}}_{4}}~{{\text{N}}^{+}}^{-}\text{OH}$ formed is completely ionized to give ${{\text{R}}_{4}}~{{\text{N}}^{+}}$and free $\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}$.

The amine Tris (trifluoro methyl) amine is found to be virtually non-basic due to the presence of three powerful electron-withdrawing $\text{C}{{\text{F}}_{3}}$ groups.

The amides are also found to be only very weakly basic in water because of the $-\text{I}$ and $-\text{R}$ effect of RCO group which makes the electron pair very slightly available on nitrogen atom.

If two $\text{C}=\text{O}$ groups are present, the resultant imides often become sufficiently acidic to form alkali metal salts. For example, benzene-1, 2-dicarboximide is not basic but is acidic in nature because of the presence of two electron-withdrawing $\text{C}=\text{O}$ groups.

When the ring contains an electron-releasing group, this reduces resonance of the $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$ group with the ring and consequently should increase basicity. The methyl group raises the electron density more at the $\text{o}-$ and $\text{p}$-positions than at the $\text{m}$-position. Thus, the carbon atom para to the methyl group has a high electron density and the lone pair on the nitrogen atom is therefore prevented to some extent from entering into resonance with the ring. A methyl group raises the electron density at the m-position is prevented less than for the p-position. Consequently, a methyl group in the ring increases the basicity of the aniline, more so from the p-position than from the m-position.

First drawing structure of

Now here a 3 carboanion is formed which is highly unstable and thus prevents there resonating structures. So at para position, methyl group discourages detocalization & hence increases the basicity.

And we know that $\text{p }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ m}$ methyl aniline are more basic than aniline. Now only o position is left.

At ortho position, there is ortho effect which increases the acidity and it is even weather base than aniline.

$\therefore $ Order becomes

Illustration

Arrange the following in increasing order of basicity

 

Solution: 

Let us see how to solve this case. Methoxy group can show both $+\text{R}\,\,\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\,\,-\text{I}$ effect. $+\text{R}$ effect will increase the basicity whereas $-I$ effect reduces basicity.

$\text{ In this case }-\text{I effect of OC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{ isstronger}\,\,\text{ }\!\!\And\!\!\text{ }\,\,\text{--I effect reduces the basicity }$

Here $\text{OC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ produces the same effect as methoxy group at para position produced, But, Ortho effect will also be here, which reduces the basic nature. hence the order is

concepts.title: Inductive effect
concepts.conceptBody:
Inductive effect

Inductive effect may be defined as the permanent displacement of electrons forming a covalent bond towards the more electronegative element or group.

The inductive effect is represented by the symbol, the arrow pointing towards the more electronegative element or group of elements. Thus in case of n-butyl chloride inductive effect may be represented as below.

Any atom or group, if attracts electrons more strongly than hydrogen, it is said to have a $-\text{I}$ effect (electro-attracting or electron-withdrawing), viz $\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}},\text{Cl},\text{Br},\text{I},\text{F},\text{COOHOHC}$, etc. while if atom or group attracts electrons less strongly than hydrogen it is said to have + I effect (electron repelling or electron releasing) viz,

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}},{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}},\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{2}}\text{CH}$ and $\text{M}{{\text{e}}_{3}}\text{C}$ groups. The important atoms or groups which cause negative or positive

Always remember this order. 

+ I (Electron-attracting) groups:

${{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{3}}\text{C}>{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\text{CH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}>\text{H}$

Acidic strength of Carboxylic acid

Compound which easily donates ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$to other are good acids. So carboxylic acids which relase $\text{H}+$ ion more easily are better acids.

Rule 

  • $-I$ effect increases acidic nature
  • $+I$ effect reduces acidic nature

For Ex.

  • $\text{HCOOH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}$

(formic acid)

Formic acid is strongest among the above 3 acids because of least $+\text{I}$ effect.

  • Now taking another example to show - I effect over acidic nature of acids

$\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{3}}\text{CCOOH}>\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\text{CHCOOH}>\text{ClC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}>\text{HCOOH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}$

More the $\text{Cl}$, more is the $-\text{I}$ effect & hence more the acidic nature.

  • In the case of $\text{Cl}$, what if $\text{Cl}$ atoms are attached for from $\text{COOH}$ group.

$\text{ClC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}>\text{ClC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}>\text{ClC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}$

The closer the -I group more is the acidic nature. This is also known as proximity effect.

  • As 's' character increases, the acidic nature increases as electronegativity of atom increases i.e.

$\text{CH}\equiv \text{C}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}-\text{COOH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}=\text{CHC}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{COOH}$

The explanation follows from the above explanation.

  • More the electronegativity to an atom, more the acidic nature. We will explain this with the help of various examples.

$\underset{(+7)}{\mathop{\text{HCl}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,>\underset{(+5)}{\mathop{\text{HCl}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,>\underset{(+3)}{\mathop{\text{HCl}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}}\,>\underset{(+1)}{\mathop{\text{HOCl}}}\,$

In this case the electronegativities of $\text{Cl}$ are in decreasing order & hence the acidic nature. Same reasoning applies to the following

$\underset{(+7)}{\mathop{\text{HCl}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,>\underset{(+6)}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}}}\,>\underset{(+5)}{\mathop{\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,$

We will deal with these kind of examples later.

Reactivity of alkyl halides

It can be deduced by reacting substance with $\text{Ag}$ salt $\left( \text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)$.

$\text{RX}+\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{AgX}$

$\text{NaCl}+\text{AgN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{fast}\text{AgCl (Ionic reactions) }$

Always keep in mind

Here would be intermediate which is most stable among other intermediates.

$\therefore \quad $ i.e. $3>2>1>\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ (If $\text{R}$ groups are difference, reactivity order)

$\text{RI}>\text{RBr}>\text{RCI}$ is the reactivity order of halogens.

On the basis of bond dissociation energy $\text{RI}$ is maximum reactive due to largest size of $\text{I}$ atom and minimum bond dissociation energy.

concepts.title: Electromeric effects
concepts.conceptBody:
Electrometric Effect

This is a temporary effect and takes place between two atoms joined by a multiple bond i.e. a double or triple bond, in the presence of an attaching reagent.

It is temporary because the molecules acquire their original electronic condition once the attacking reagent is removed.

It also occurs as$+\text{E}$ and $-\text{E}$ effect, similar to inductive effect.

  • $-$E effect: When the transfer of electrons take place away from attacking reagent.

  • +E effect: When the transfer takes place towards the attacking agent.

Interesting case

For case 1 

For case 2 

Case 1 will be the result, because it produces 2 carbocation which is more stable.

Note: It is a misconception that adding a nucleophile will cause

–E effect & electrophile causes $+\text{E}$ effect. You can see from the above question that electrophile caused - E effect.

concepts.title: Properties of carbocation
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties of Carbocations

(i) Rearrangement 

The bonding electrons of a carbocation may shift between adjacent atoms to form a more stable carbocation. For example rearrangement will occur if a secondary carbocation can be formed from a primary carbocation because a secondary carbocation is more stable than a primary carbocation for e.g.

Phenyl shifts are preferable as they increase the stability by resonance effect The migration aptitude of various groups is

(a) ${{\text{H}}^{-}}>3>2>1$ alkyl $>\text{CH}_{3}^{-}$

(b) $\text{P}{{\text{h}}^{-}}>\text{CH}_{3}^{-}$

However a migration in reverse direction can also take place if the migration leads to formation of benzylic compound which is highly stable.

The result is highly stable due to delocalization of electrons due to resonance.

Another such example is

1. SPECIAL CASES:

So for major product in such cases, we go by migratory aptitude, and phenyl shift is preferable over methide shift. So compound B will be major product.

2. Ring expansion

Rearrangement may lead to a change in ring size or formation of a ring causing greater stability for example

The ring stability increase as number of carbon atoms in the ring increases.

3 < 4 < 5 < 6

increasing order of stability

Generally ring carrying more than 6 carbon atoms are less stable.

3. 

lone pair at Oxygen will react with hydrogen to complete its octet \& leave the compound giving us a 2 carbocation.

Now compound (A) has 2 option

(c) & (f) are the major products as more substituted alkenes are more stable.

4. Deprotonation

Carbocations are very reactive species and are very strongly driven to dispose of the positive charge. Even a weak base such a water can accomplish this deprotonation.

Vinylic carbocations generally do not rearrange, even if they can become more stable. For example, the rearrangement shown below does not occur, even though a secondary carbocation would rearrange to become more stable allylic carbocation (primary with resonance).

This resistance to rearrangement is probably due to orbital alignment restrictions during the rearrangement transition state.
